perturbation.original_text: During each year of this agreement, Dr. Murray will be paid no later than the 30st day of January based on the cumulative Net Sales of the Dr. Murray Products for the preceding 12 months based upon the following scale: Net Sales per 12 Months Royalty $2,000,001 to $3,500,000 $150,000 $3,500,001 to $5,000,000 $250,000 $5,000,001 to $10,000,000 $350,000 $10,000,001 to $25,000,000 $500,000 $25,000,001 to $50,000,000 $1,000,000 $50,000,001 to $75,000,000 $1,500,000 $75,000,001 to $100,000,000 $2,000,000 Greater than $100,000,000 $2,500,000
perturbation.changed_text: During each year of this agreement, Dr. Murray will be paid at the discretion of EHN, based on their assessment of the Net Sales of the Dr. Murray Products for the preceding 12 months. The royalty will be determined according to the following scale: Net Sales per 12 Months Royalty $2,000,001 to $3,500,000 $150,000 $3,500,001 to $5,000,000 $250,000 $5,000,001 to $10,000,000 $350,000 $10,000,001 to $25,000,000 $500,000 $25,000,001 to $50,000,000 $1,000,000 $50,000,001 to $75,000,000 $1,500,000 $75,000,001 to $100,000,000 $2,000,000 Greater than $100,000,000 $2,500,000
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will be paid no later than the 30st day of January' to 'will be paid at the discretion of EHN' creates uncertainty and makes the royalty payment discretionary, contradicting the intent of a fixed royalty agreement. It creates ambiguity as to the timing and obligation of payment, even if the calculation is based on a fixed scale.
perturbation.law_explanation: Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-353 mandates that wages or compensation agreed upon must be paid when due. By making the payment 'at the discretion of EHN,' the modified contract language contradicts this statute, as it implies that EHN can choose when, or if, to pay the agreed-upon royalties, even if net sales targets are met.

DISCLAIMER This online version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is primarily maintained for legislative drafting purposes and reflects the version of law that is effective on January 1st of the year following the most recent legislative session. The official version of the Arizona Revised Statutes is published by Thomson Reuters. ARS Title Page Previous Section Title 23 Next Section Printable Version 23-353 - Payment of wages of discharged employee; violation; classification 23-353 . Payment of wages of discharged employee; violation; classification A. When an employee is discharged from the service of an employer, he shall be paid wages due him within seven working days or the end of the next regular pay period, whichever is sooner. B. When an employee quits the service of an employer he shall be paid in the usual manner all wages due him no later than the regular payday for the pay period during which the termination occurred. If requested by the employee, such wages shall be paid by mail. C. Every employer, including this state and its political subdivisions, shall pay wages or compensation due an employee under this section in lawful money of the United States by negotiable check, draft, money order or warrant, in the case of the state or any political subdivision, which can be immediately redeemed in cash at a bank or other financial institution, payable on demand or by deposit in a financial institution of employee's choice and dated not later than the day upon which the check, draft, money order or warrant is given, and not otherwise. D. A person violating this section is guilty of a petty offense. perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces a contradiction. The original contract specifies a fixed payment schedule ('will be paid no later than the 30st day of January'), while the changed version makes the payment discretionary ('will be paid at the discretion of EHN'). This contradicts Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-353, which mandates that wages or compensation agreed upon must be paid when due. While the statute refers to 'wages of discharged employee,' the Law Explanation extends this principle to royalties. By allowing EHN to decide when or if to pay, the changed text undermines the legal obligation to pay the agreed-upon royalties, even if net sales targets are met. Scraped Law Snippet 1 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 both contain the text of Arizona Revised Statutes § 23-353, which states that when an employee is discharged, 'he shall be paid wages due him.' The change from a fixed payment date to discretionary payment directly contradicts the intent of this statute, as payment becomes uncertain.

perturbation.original_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, EHS or EHN may immediately terminate the Services Term without notice should Dr. Murray be in breach of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Notwithstanding the foregoing, EHS or EHN may terminate the Services Term at any time without notice for any reason whatsoever.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the termination clause to allow for termination 'at any time without notice for any reason whatsoever' significantly weakens Dr. Murray's position and eliminates the requirement of a breach of contract. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in most contracts and is not aligned with standard contract law principles, potentially leading to unenforceability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205 establishes the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing in every contract. The modified termination clause, allowing termination for any reason without notice, arguably violates this covenant by depriving Dr. Murray of the benefit of his bargain and acting in bad faith. Although Arizona recognizes at-will employment, this is a service contract, not an at-will employment arrangement, so the principle still applies. The UCC citation is included as this covenant is found throughout contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows EHS or EHN to terminate the agreement "at any time without notice for any reason whatsoever," which directly contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing "honest dealing" and requiring "faithful performance of duties" and "observance of fair dealing standards." The modified termination clause permits termination for any reason, which could include dishonest or unfair reasons, violating this covenant. Further, Scraped Law Snippet 2 states that "Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement." The change eliminates any requirement for a legitimate reason to terminate, thus allowing for potential bad faith termination, contradicting the UCC's requirement of good faith in contract performance.

perturbation.original_text: The parties shall first attempt to resolve any dispute related to this Agreement in an amicable manner by mediation conducted in Phoenix, Arizona. The mediation shall take place no more than sixty (60) days after written notice from the party requesting such mediation. Any disputes remaining unresolved after mediation shall be settled by binding arbitration conducted in Phoenix, Arizona under the Commercial Arbitration Rules of the American Arbitration Association (AAA).
perturbation.changed_text: The parties may, at their sole discretion, attempt to resolve any dispute related to this Agreement in an amicable manner by non-binding mediation conducted wherever EHS or EHN chooses. Any disputes remaining unresolved after mediation will be subject to litigation in a venue of EHS or EHN’s choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'shall first attempt' to 'may, at their sole discretion, attempt' removes the mandatory requirement for mediation. Switching from 'binding arbitration' to 'litigation' favors EHS/EHN. The clause stating the venue for litigation would be in a venue of EHS or EHN’s choosing makes it more onerous for Dr. Murray and less fair. This removes protections typically afforded under arbitration agreements, and creates a potentially unfair power imbalance.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Federal Arbitration Act (9 U.S.C. § 2) favors and enforces valid arbitration agreements. By removing the mandatory arbitration clause and replacing it with litigation at a venue of EHS or EHN's choosing, the modified language undermines the principle of arbitration as a fair and efficient dispute resolution method. While not directly prohibiting arbitration, it discourages it in bad faith, since EHS or EHN could simply choose to litigate in a distant or inconvenient location.

perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts the principle of enforcing arbitration agreements, potentially violating the Federal Arbitration Act, which favors arbitration. By changing 'shall first attempt' to 'may, at their sole discretion, attempt' and replacing binding arbitration with litigation at a venue of EHS or EHN's choosing, the contract circumvents arbitration. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 9 USC 2, affirms the 'validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate.' The modification weakens the initial agreement to arbitrate, creating an imbalance and undermining the act. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.
